Output
"{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Discrete Fourier Transforms\\n3: \\n4: Routines in this module:\\n5: \\n6: fft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n7: ifft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n8: rfft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n9: irfft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n10: hfft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n11: ihfft(a, n=None, axis=-1, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n12: fftn(a, s=None, axes=None, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n13: ifftn(a, s=None, axes=None, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n14: rfftn(a, s=None, axes=None, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n15: irfftn(a, s=None, axes=None, norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n16: fft2(a, s=None, axes=(-2,-1), norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n17: ifft2(a, s=None, axes=(-2, -1), norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n18: rfft2(a, s=None, axes=(-2,-1), norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n19: irfft2(a, s=None, axes=(-2, -1), norm=\\\"backward\\\")\\n20: \\n21: i = inverse transform\\n22: r = transform of purely real data\\n23: h = Hermite transform\\n24: n = n-dimensional transform\\n25: 2 = 2-dimensional transform\\n26: (Note: 2D routines are just nD routines with different default\\n27: behavior.)\\n28: \\n29: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n30: __all__ = ['fft', 'ifft', 'rfft', 'irfft', 'hfft', 'ihfft', 'rfftn',\\n31:            'irfftn', 'rfft2', 'irfft2', 'fft2', 'ifft2', 'fftn', 'ifftn']\\n32: \\n33: import functools\\n34: import warnings\\n35: \\n36: from numpy.lib.array_utils import normalize_axis_index\\n37: from numpy._core import (asarray, empty_like, result_type,\\n38:                          conjugate, take, sqrt, reciprocal)\\n39: from . import _pocketfft_umath as pfu\\n40: from numpy._core import overrides\\n41: \\n42: \\n43: array_function_dispatch = functools.partial(\\n44:     overrides.array_function_dispatch, module='numpy.fft')\\n45: \\n46: \\n47: # `inv_norm` is a float by which the result of the transform needs to be\\n48: # divided. This replaces the original, more intuitive 'fct` parameter to avoid\\n49: # divisions by zero (or alternatively additional checks) in the case of\\n50: # zero-length axes during its computation.\\n51: def _raw_fft(a, n, axis, is_real, is_forward, norm, out=None):\\n52:     if n < 1:\\n53:         raise ValueError(f\\\"Invalid number of FFT data points ({n}) specified.\\\")\\n54: \\n55:     # Calculate the normalization factor, passing in the array dtype to\\n56:     # avoid precision loss in the possible sqrt or reciprocal.\\n57:     if not is_forward:\\n58:         norm = _swap_direction(norm)\\n59: \\n60:     real_dtype = result_type(a.real.dtype, 1.0)\\n61:     if norm is None or norm == \\\"backward\\\":\\n62:         fct = 1\\n63:     elif norm == \\\"ortho\\\":\\n64:         fct = reciprocal(sqrt(n, dtype=real_dtype))\\n65:     elif norm == \\\"forward\\\":\\n66:         fct = reciprocal(n, dtype=real_dtype)\\n67:     else:\\n68:         raise ValueError(f'Invalid norm value {norm}; should be \\\"backward\\\",'\\n69:                          '\\\"ortho\\\" or \\\"forward\\\".')\\n70: \\n71:     n_out = n\\n72:     if is_real:\\n73:         if is_forward:\\n74:             ufunc = pfu.rfft_n_even if n % 2 == 0 else pfu.rfft_n_odd\\n75:             n_out = n // 2 + 1\\n76:         else:\\n77:             ufunc = pfu.irfft\\n78:     else:\\n79:         ufunc = pfu.fft if is_forward else pfu.ifft\\n80: \\n81:     axis = normalize_axis_index(axis, a.ndim)\\n82: \\n83:     if out is None:\\n84:         if is_real and not is_forward:  # irfft, complex in, real output.\\n85:             out_dtype = real_dtype\\n86:         else:  # Others, complex output.\\n87:             out_dtype = result_type(a.dtype, 1j)\\n88:         out = empty_like(a, shape=a.shape[:axis] + (n_out,) + a.shape[axis+1:],\\n89:                          dtype=out_dtype)\\n90:     elif ((shape := getattr(out, \\\"shape\\\", None)) is not None\\n91:           and (len(shape) != a.ndim or shape[axis] != n_out)):\\n92:         raise ValueError(\\\"output array has wrong shape.\\\")\\n93: \\n94:     return ufunc(a, fct, axes=[(axis,), (), (axis,)], out=out)\\n95: \\n96: \\n97: _SWAP_DIRECTION_MAP = {\\\"backward\\\": \\\"forward\\\", None: \\\"forward\\\",\\n98:                        \\\"ortho\\\": \\\"ortho\\\", \\\"forward\\\": \\\"backward\\\"}\\n99: \\n100: \\n101: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/fft/_pocketfft.py%3FL1-100\"}"
43. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "cat > /workspace/download_and_rename_files.py << 'ENDSCRIPT'\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nScript to automate downloading and renaming array_function_dispatch\nThis will be used to process all files systematically\n\"\"\"\n\nimport re\nimport json\n\ndef rename_dispatch(content):\n    \"\"\"Apply all necessary renames to content.\"\"\"\n    \n    # 1. Replace import statements\n    content = re.sub(\n        r'from numpy\\._core\\.overrides import array_function_dispatch',\n        r'from numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function',\n        content\n    )\n    \n    # 2. Replace in functools.partial\n    content = re.sub(\n        r'overrides\\.array_function_dispatch',\n        r'overrides.dispatch_array_function',\n        content\n    )\n    \n    # 3. Replace local alias assignments like:\n    # array_function_dispatch = functools.partial(...)\n    # But only when it's a module-level assignment\n    content = re.sub(\n        r'^\\s*array_function_dispatch = functools\\.partial',\n        lambda m: m.group(0).replace('array_function_dispatch', 'dispatch_array_function'),\n        content,\n        flags=re.MULTILINE\n    )\n    \n    # 4. Replace decorator usages @array_function_dispatch\n    content = re.sub(\n        r'@array_function_dispatch\\(',\n        r'@dispatch_array_function(',\n        content\n    )\n    \n    # 5. Replace remaining standalone occurrences\n    # This handles cases like: array_function_dispatch = ...\n    # and @array_function_dispatch\n    content = re.sub(\n        r'\\barray_function_dispatch\\b',\n        r'dispatch_array_function',\n        content\n    )\n    \n    return content\n\n\nFILES_INFO = {\n    \"numpy/_core/numeric.py\": \"Large file - process in sections\",\n    \"numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py\": \"Large file - process in sections\", \n    \"numpy/_core/function_base.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/_core/shape_base.py\": \"Contains 3 instances\",\n    \"numpy/_core/defchararray.py\": \"Contains 3 instances\",\n    \"numpy/linalg/_linalg.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/fft/_pocketfft.py\": \"Contains 3 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_npyio_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_polynomial_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_shape_base_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_type_check_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_histograms_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_arraysetops_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_twodim_base_impl.py\": \"Contains 2 instances\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_ufunclike_impl.py\": \"Contains direct import and usage\",\n    \"numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py\": \"Contains import and usage\",\n    \"numpy/lib/recfunctions.py\": \"Contains import and usage\",\n    \"benchmarks/benchmarks/bench_overrides.py\": \"Contains import and wrapper function\",\n    \"numpy/_core/tests/test_overrides.py\": \"Test file - contains import and usage\",\n}\n\nprint(\"File Processing Configuration\")\nprint(\"=\" * 60)\nfor filepath, notes in FILES_INFO.items():\n    print(f\"{filepath}\")\n    print(f\"  -> {notes}\")\nprint(\"=\" * 60)\nprint(f\"\\nTotal files to process: {len(FILES_INFO)}\")\nENDSCRIPT\n\npython3 /workspace/download_and_rename_files.py\n"
}

{
  "path": "numpy/lib/_ufunclike_impl.py",

Output
"{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Module of functions that are like ufuncs in acting on arrays and optionally\\n3: storing results in an output array.\\n4: \\n5: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n6: __all__ = ['fix', 'isneginf', 'isposinf']\\n7: \\n8: import numpy._core.numeric as nx\\n9: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch\\n10: \\n11: \\n12: def _dispatcher(x, out=None):\\n13:     return (x, out)\\n14: \\n15: \\n16: @array_function_dispatch(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\\n17: def fix(x, out=None):\\n18:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n19:     Round to nearest integer towards zero.\\n20: \\n21:     Round an array of floats element-wise to nearest integer towards zero.\\n22:     The rounded values have the same data-type as the input.\\n23: \\n24:     Parameters\\n25:     ----------\\n26:     x : array_like\\n27:         An array to be rounded\\n28:     out : ndarray, optional\\n29:         A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have\\n30:         a shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\\n31:         freshly-allocated array is returned.\\n32: \\n33:     Returns\\n34:     -------\\n35:     out : ndarray of floats\\n36:         An array with the same dimensions and data-type as the input.\\n37:         If second argument is not supplied then a new array is returned\\n38:         with the rounded values.\\n39: \\n40:         If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there.\\n41:         The return value ``out`` is then a reference to that array.\\n42: \\n43:     See Also\\n44:     --------\\n45:     rint, trunc, floor, ceil\\n46:     around : Round to given number of decimals\\n47: \\n48:     Examples\\n49:     --------\\n50:     >>> import numpy as np\\n51:     >>> np.fix(3.14)\\n52:     3.0\\n53:     >>> np.fix(3)\\n54:     3\\n55:     >>> np.fix([2.1, 2.9, -2.1, -2.9])\\n56:     array([ 2.,  2., -2., -2.])\\n57: \\n58:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n59:     # promote back to an array if flattened\\n60:     res = nx.asanyarray(nx.ceil(x, out=out))\\n61:     res = nx.floor(x, out=res, where=nx.greater_equal(x, 0))\\n62: \\n63:     # when no out argument is passed and no subclasses are involved, flatten\\n64:     # scalars\\n65:     if out is None and type(res) is nx.ndarray:\\n66:         res = res[()]\\n67:     return res\\n68: \\n69: \\n70: @array_function_dispatch(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\\n71: def isposinf(x, out=None):\\n72:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n73:     Test element-wise for positive infinity, return result as bool array.\\n74: \\n75:     Parameters\\n76:     ----------\\n77:     x : array_like\\n78:         The input array.\\n79:     out : array_like, optional\\n80:         A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have a\\n81:         shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\\n82:         freshly-allocated boolean array is returned.\\n83: \\n84:     Returns\\n85:     -------\\n86:     out : ndarray\\n87:         A boolean array with the same dimensions as the input.\\n88:         If second argument is not supplied then a boolean array is returned\\n89:         with values True where the corresponding element of the input is\\n90:         positive infinity and values False where the element of the input is\\n91:         not positive infinity.\\n92: \\n93:         If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there. If the\\n94:         type of that array is a numeric type the result is represented as zeros\\n95:         and ones, if the type is boolean then as False and True.\\n96:         The return value `out` is then a reference to that array.\\n97: \\n98:     See Also\\n99:     --------\\n100:     isinf, isneginf, isfinite, isnan\\n101: \\n102:     Notes\\n103:     -----\\n104:     NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic\\n105:     (IEEE 754).\\n106: \\n107:     Errors result if the second argument is also supplied when x is a scalar\\n108:     input, if first and second arguments have different shapes, or if the\\n109:     first argument has complex values\\n110: \\n111:     Examples\\n112:     --------\\n113:     >>> import numpy as np\\n114:     >>> np.isposinf(np.inf)\\n115:     True\\n116:     >>> np.isposinf(-np.inf)\\n117:     False\\n118:     >>> np.isposinf([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\\n119:     array([False, False,  True])\\n120: \\n121:     >>> x = np.array([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\\n122:     >>> y = np.array([2, 2, 2])\\n123:     >>> np.isposinf(x, y)\\n124:     array([0, 0, 1])\\n125:     >>> y\\n126:     array([0, 0, 1])\\n127: \\n128:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n129:     is_inf = nx.isinf(x)\\n130:     try:\\n131:         signbit = ~nx.signbit(x)\\n132:     except TypeError as e:\\n133:         dtype = nx.asanyarray(x).dtype\\n134:         raise TypeError(f'This operation is not supported for {dtype} values '\\n135:                         'because it would be ambiguous.') from e\\n136:     else:\\n137:         return nx.logical_and(is_inf, signbit, out)\\n138: \\n139: \\n140: @array_function_dispatch(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\\n141: def isneginf(x, out=None):\\n142:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n143:     Test element-wise for negative infinity, return result as bool array.\\n144: \\n145:     Parameters\\n146:     ----------\\n147:     x : array_like\\n148:         The input array.\\n149:     out : array_like, optional\\n150:         A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have a\\n151:         shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\\n152:         freshly-allocated boolean array is returned.\\n153: \\n154:     Returns\\n155:     -------\\n156:     out : ndarray\\n157:         A boolean array with the same dimensions as the input.\\n158:         If second argument is not supplied then a numpy boolean array is\\n159:         returned with values True where the corresponding element of the\\n160:         input is negative infinity and values False where the element of\\n161:         the input is not negative infinity.\\n162: \\n163:         If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there. If the\\n164:         type of that array is a numeric type the result is represented as\\n165:         zeros and ones, if the type is boolean then as False and True. The\\n166:         return value `out` is then a reference to that array.\\n167: \\n168:     See Also\\n169:     --------\\n170:     isinf, isposinf, isnan, isfinite\\n171: \\n172:     Notes\\n173:     -----\\n174:     NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic\\n175:     (IEEE 754).\\n176: \\n177:     Errors result if the second argument is also supplied when x is a scalar\\n178:     input, if first and second arguments have different shapes, or if the\\n179:     first argument has complex values.\\n180: \\n181:     Examples\\n182:     --------\\n183:     >>> import numpy as np\\n184:     >>> np.isneginf(-np.inf)\\n185:     True\\n186:     >>> np.isneginf(np.inf)\\n187:     False\\n188:     >>> np.isneginf([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\\n189:     array([ True, False, False])\\n190: \\n191:     >>> x = np.array([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\\n192:     >>> y = np.array([2, 2, 2])\\n193:     >>> np.isneginf(x, y)\\n194:     array([1, 0, 0])\\n195:     >>> y\\n196:     array([1, 0, 0])\\n197: \\n198:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n199:     is_inf = nx.isinf(x)\\n200:     try:\\n201:         signbit = nx.signbit(x)\\n202:     except TypeError as e:\\n203:         dtype = nx.asanyarray(x).dtype\\n204:         raise TypeError(f'This operation is not supported for {dtype} values '\\n205:                         'because it would be ambiguous.') from e\\n206:     else:\\n207:         return nx.logical_and(is_inf, signbit, out)\\n208: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_ufunclike_impl.py\"}"
45. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_read_file @ -
Input
{
  "path": "numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py",
  "repo": "github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2"
}
Output
"{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Wrapper functions to more user-friendly calling of certain math functions\\n3: whose output data-type is different than the input data-type in certain\\n4: domains of the input.\\n5: \\n6: For example, for functions like `log` with branch cuts, the versions in this\\n7: module provide the mathematically valid answers in the complex plane::\\n8: \\n9:   >>> import math\\n10:   >>> np.emath.log(-math.exp(1)) == (1+1j*math.pi)\\n11:   True\\n12: \\n13: Similarly, `sqrt`, other base logarithms, `power` and trig functions are\\n14: correctly handled.  See their respective docstrings for specific examples.\\n15: \\n16: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n17: import numpy._core.numeric as nx\\n18: import numpy._core.numerictypes as nt\\n19: from numpy._core.numeric import asarray, any\\n20: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch, set_module\\n21: from numpy.lib._type_check_impl import isreal\\n22: \\n23: \\n24: __all__ = [\\n25:     'sqrt', 'log', 'log2', 'logn', 'log10', 'power', 'arccos', 'arcsin',\\n26:     'arctanh'\\n27:     ]\\n28: \\n29: \\n30: _ln2 = nx.log(2.0)\\n31: \\n32: \\n33: def _tocomplex(arr):\\n34:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Convert its input `arr` to a complex array.\\n35: \\n36:     The input is returned as a complex array of the smallest type that will fit\\n37:     the original data: types like single, byte, short, etc. become csingle,\\n38:     while others become cdouble.\\n39: \\n40:     A copy of the input is always made.\\n41: \\n42:     Parameters\\n43:     ----------\\n44:     arr : array\\n45: \\n46:     Returns\\n47:     -------\\n48:     array\\n49:         An array with the same input data as the input but in complex form.\\n50: \\n51:     Examples\\n52:     --------\\n53:     >>> import numpy as np\\n54: \\n55:     First, consider an input of type short:\\n56: \\n57:     >>> a = np.array([1,2,3],np.short)\\n58: \\n59:     >>> ac = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(a); ac\\n60:     array([1.+0.j, 2.+0.j, 3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\\n61: \\n62:     >>> ac.dtype\\n63:     dtype('complex64')\\n64: \\n65:     If the input is of type double, the output is correspondingly of the\\n66:     complex double type as well:\\n67: \\n68:     >>> b = np.array([1,2,3],np.double)\\n69: \\n70:     >>> bc = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(b); bc\\n71:     array([1.+0.j, 2.+0.j, 3.+0.j])\\n72: \\n73:     >>> bc.dtype\\n74:     dtype('complex128')\\n75: \\n76:     Note that even if the input was complex to begin with, a copy is still\\n77:     made, since the astype() method always copies:\\n78: \\n79:     >>> c = np.array([1,2,3],np.csingle)\\n80: \\n81:     >>> cc = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(c); cc\\n82:     array([1.+0.j,  2.+0.j,  3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\\n83: \\n84:     >>> c *= 2; c\\n85:     array([2.+0.j,  4.+0.j,  6.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\\n86: \\n87:     >>> cc\\n88:     array([1.+0.j,  2.+0.j,  3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\\n89:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n90:     if issubclass(arr.dtype.type, (nt.single, nt.byte, nt.short, nt.ubyte,\\n91:                                    nt.ushort, nt.csingle)):\\n92:         return arr.astype(nt.csingle)\\n93:     else:\\n94:         return arr.astype(nt.cdouble)\\n95: \\n96: \\n97: def _fix_real_lt_zero(x):\\n98:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Convert `x` to complex if it has real, negative components.\\n99: \\n100:     Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\\n101: \\n102:     Parameters\\n103:     ----------\\n104:     x : array_like\\n105: \\n106:     Returns\\n107:     -------\\n108:     array\\n109: \\n110:     Examples\\n111:     --------\\n112:     >>> import numpy as np\\n113:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_lt_zero([1,2])\\n114:     array([1, 2])\\n115: \\n116:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_lt_zero([-1,2])\\n117:     array([-1.+0.j,  2.+0.j])\\n118: \\n119:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n120:     x = asarray(x)\\n121:     if any(isreal(x) & (x < 0)):\\n122:         x = _tocomplex(x)\\n123:     return x\\n124: \\n125: \\n126: def _fix_int_lt_zero(x):\\n127:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Convert `x` to double if it has real, negative components.\\n128: \\n129:     Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\\n130: \\n131:     Parameters\\n132:     ----------\\n133:     x : array_like\\n134: \\n135:     Returns\\n136:     -------\\n137:     array\\n138: \\n139:     Examples\\n140:     --------\\n141:     >>> import numpy as np\\n142:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_int_lt_zero([1,2])\\n143:     array([1, 2])\\n144: \\n145:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_int_lt_zero([-1,2])\\n146:     array([-1.,  2.])\\n147:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n148:     x = asarray(x)\\n149:     if any(isreal(x) & (x < 0)):\\n150:         x = x * 1.0\\n151:     return x\\n152: \\n153: \\n154: def _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x):\\n155:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Convert `x` to complex if it has real components x_i with abs(x_i)>1.\\n156: \\n157:     Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\\n158: \\n159:     Parameters\\n160:     ----------\\n161:     x : array_like\\n162: \\n163:     Returns\\n164:     -------\\n165:     array\\n166: \\n167:     Examples\\n168:     --------\\n169:     >>> import numpy as np\\n170:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_abs_gt_1([0,1])\\n171:     array([0, 1])\\n172: \\n173:     >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_abs_gt_1([0,2])\\n174:     array([0.+0.j, 2.+0.j])\\n175:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n176:     x = asarray(x)\\n177:     if any(isreal(x) & (abs(x) > 1)):\\n178:         x = _tocomplex(x)\\n179:     return x\\n180: \\n181: \\n182: def _unary_dispatcher(x):\\n183:     return (x,)\\n184: \\n185: \\n186: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n187: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n188: def sqrt(x):\\n189:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n190:     Compute the square root of x.\\n191: \\n192:     For negative input elements, a complex value is returned\\n193:     (unlike `numpy.sqrt` which returns NaN).\\n194: \\n195:     Parameters\\n196:     ----------\\n197:     x : array_like\\n198:        The input value(s).\\n199: \\n200:     Returns\\n201:     -------\\n202:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n203:        The square root of `x`. If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\\n204:        otherwise an array is returned.\\n205: \\n206:     See Also\\n207:     --------\\n208:     numpy.sqrt\\n209: \\n210:     Examples\\n211:     --------\\n212:     For real, non-negative inputs this works just like `numpy.sqrt`:\\n213: \\n214:     >>> import numpy as np\\n215: \\n216:     >>> np.emath.sqrt(1)\\n217:     1.0\\n218:     >>> np.emath.sqrt([1, 4])\\n219:     array([1.,  2.])\\n220: \\n221:     But it automatically handles negative inputs:\\n222: \\n223:     >>> np.emath.sqrt(-1)\\n224:     1j\\n225:     >>> np.emath.sqrt([-1,4])\\n226:     array([0.+1.j, 2.+0.j])\\n227: \\n228:     Different results are expected because:\\n229:     floating point 0.0 and -0.0 are distinct.\\n230: \\n231:     For more control, explicitly use complex() as follows:\\n232: \\n233:     >>> np.emath.sqrt(complex(-4.0, 0.0))\\n234:     2j\\n235:     >>> np.emath.sqrt(complex(-4.0, -0.0))\\n236:     -2j\\n237:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n238:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n239:     return nx.sqrt(x)\\n240: \\n241: \\n242: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n243: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n244: def log(x):\\n245:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n246:     Compute the natural logarithm of `x`.\\n247: \\n248:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see `numpy.log`)\\n249:     of :math:`log_e(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this is a real number (``log(0)``\\n250:     returns ``-inf`` and ``log(np.inf)`` returns ``inf``). Otherwise, the\\n251:     complex principle value is returned.\\n252: \\n253:     Parameters\\n254:     ----------\\n255:     x : array_like\\n256:        The value(s) whose log is (are) required.\\n257: \\n258:     Returns\\n259:     -------\\n260:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n261:        The log of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\\n262:        otherwise an array is returned.\\n263: \\n264:     See Also\\n265:     --------\\n266:     numpy.log\\n267: \\n268:     Notes\\n269:     -----\\n270:     For a log() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log`\\n271:     (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log` and this `log` are identical,\\n272:     i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`, and,\\n273:     notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\\n274: \\n275:     Examples\\n276:     --------\\n277:     >>> import numpy as np\\n278:     >>> np.emath.log(np.exp(1))\\n279:     1.0\\n280: \\n281:     Negative arguments are handled \\\"correctly\\\" (recall that\\n282:     ``exp(log(x)) == x`` does *not* hold for real ``x < 0``):\\n283: \\n284:     >>> np.emath.log(-np.exp(1)) == (1 + np.pi * 1j)\\n285:     True\\n286: \\n287:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n288:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n289:     return nx.log(x)\\n290: \\n291: \\n292: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n293: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n294: def log10(x):\\n295:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n296:     Compute the logarithm base 10 of `x`.\\n297: \\n298:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n299:     `numpy.log10`) of :math:`log_{10}(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this\\n300:     is a real number (``log10(0)`` returns ``-inf`` and ``log10(np.inf)``\\n301:     returns ``inf``). Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\\n302: \\n303:     Parameters\\n304:     ----------\\n305:     x : array_like or scalar\\n306:        The value(s) whose log base 10 is (are) required.\\n307: \\n308:     Returns\\n309:     -------\\n310:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n311:        The log base 10 of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\\n312:        otherwise an array object is returned.\\n313: \\n314:     See Also\\n315:     --------\\n316:     numpy.log10\\n317: \\n318:     Notes\\n319:     -----\\n320:     For a log10() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log10`\\n321:     (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log10` and this `log10` are\\n322:     identical, i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`,\\n323:     and, notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\\n324: \\n325:     Examples\\n326:     --------\\n327:     >>> import numpy as np\\n328: \\n329:     (We set the printing precision so the example can be auto-tested)\\n330: \\n331:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n332: \\n333:     >>> np.emath.log10(10**1)\\n334:     1.0\\n335: \\n336:     >>> np.emath.log10([-10**1, -10**2, 10**2])\\n337:     array([1.+1.3644j, 2.+1.3644j, 2.+0.j    ])\\n338: \\n339:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n340:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n341:     return nx.log10(x)\\n342: \\n343: \\n344: def _logn_dispatcher(n, x):\\n345:     return (n, x,)\\n346: \\n347: \\n348: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n349: @array_function_dispatch(_logn_dispatcher)\\n350: def logn(n, x):\\n351:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n352:     Take log base n of x.\\n353: \\n354:     If `x` contains negative inputs, the answer is computed and returned in the\\n355:     complex domain.\\n356: \\n357:     Parameters\\n358:     ----------\\n359:     n : array_like\\n360:        The integer base(s) in which the log is taken.\\n361:     x : array_like\\n362:        The value(s) whose log base `n` is (are) required.\\n363: \\n364:     Returns\\n365:     -------\\n366:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n367:        The log base `n` of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is\\n368:        `out`, otherwise an array is returned.\\n369: \\n370:     Examples\\n371:     --------\\n372:     >>> import numpy as np\\n373:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n374: \\n375:     >>> np.emath.logn(2, [4, 8])\\n376:     array([2., 3.])\\n377:     >>> np.emath.logn(2, [-4, -8, 8])\\n378:     array([2.+4.5324j, 3.+4.5324j, 3.+0.j    ])\\n379: \\n380:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n381:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n382:     n = _fix_real_lt_zero(n)\\n383:     return nx.log(x)/nx.log(n)\\n384: \\n385: \\n386: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n387: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n388: def log2(x):\\n389:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n390:     Compute the logarithm base 2 of `x`.\\n391: \\n392:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n393:     `numpy.log2`) of :math:`log_2(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this is\\n394:     a real number (``log2(0)`` returns ``-inf`` and ``log2(np.inf)`` returns\\n395:     ``inf``). Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\\n396: \\n397:     Parameters\\n398:     ----------\\n399:     x : array_like\\n400:        The value(s) whose log base 2 is (are) required.\\n401: \\n402:     Returns\\n403:     -------\\n404:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n405:        The log base 2 of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\\n406:        otherwise an array is returned.\\n407: \\n408:     See Also\\n409:     --------\\n410:     numpy.log2\\n411: \\n412:     Notes\\n413:     -----\\n414:     For a log2() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log2`\\n415:     (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log2` and this `log2` are\\n416:     identical, i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`,\\n417:     and, notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\\n418: \\n419:     Examples\\n420:     --------\\n421: \\n422:     We set the printing precision so the example can be auto-tested:\\n423: \\n424:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n425: \\n426:     >>> np.emath.log2(8)\\n427:     3.0\\n428:     >>> np.emath.log2([-4, -8, 8])\\n429:     array([2.+4.5324j, 3.+4.5324j, 3.+0.j    ])\\n430: \\n431:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n432:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n433:     return nx.log2(x)\\n434: \\n435: \\n436: def _power_dispatcher(x, p):\\n437:     return (x, p)\\n438: \\n439: \\n440: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n441: @array_function_dispatch(_power_dispatcher)\\n442: def power(x, p):\\n443:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n444:     Return x to the power p, (x**p).\\n445: \\n446:     If `x` contains negative values, the output is converted to the\\n447:     complex domain.\\n448: \\n449:     Parameters\\n450:     ----------\\n451:     x : array_like\\n452:         The input value(s).\\n453:     p : array_like of ints\\n454:         The power(s) to which `x` is raised. If `x` contains multiple values,\\n455:         `p` has to either be a scalar, or contain the same number of values\\n456:         as `x`. In the latter case, the result is\\n457:         ``x[0]**p[0], x[1]**p[1], ...``.\\n458: \\n459:     Returns\\n460:     -------\\n461:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n462:         The result of ``x**p``. If `x` and `p` are scalars, so is `out`,\\n463:         otherwise an array is returned.\\n464: \\n465:     See Also\\n466:     --------\\n467:     numpy.power\\n468: \\n469:     Examples\\n470:     --------\\n471:     >>> import numpy as np\\n472:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n473: \\n474:     >>> np.emath.power(2, 2)\\n475:     4\\n476: \\n477:     >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], 2)\\n478:     array([ 4, 16])\\n479: \\n480:     >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], -2)\\n481:     array([0.25  ,  0.0625])\\n482: \\n483:     >>> np.emath.power([-2, 4], 2)\\n484:     array([ 4.-0.j, 16.+0.j])\\n485: \\n486:     >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], [2, 4])\\n487:     array([ 4, 256])\\n488: \\n489:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n490:     x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\\n491:     p = _fix_int_lt_zero(p)\\n492:     return nx.power(x, p)\\n493: \\n494: \\n495: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n496: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n497: def arccos(x):\\n498:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n499:     Compute the inverse cosine of x.\\n500: \\n501:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n502:     `numpy.arccos`) of the inverse cosine of `x`. For real `x` such that\\n503:     `abs(x) <= 1`, this is a real number in the closed interval\\n504:     :math:`[0, \\\\\\\\pi]`.  Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\\n505: \\n506:     Parameters\\n507:     ----------\\n508:     x : array_like or scalar\\n509:        The value(s) whose arccos is (are) required.\\n510: \\n511:     Returns\\n512:     -------\\n513:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n514:        The inverse cosine(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so\\n515:        is `out`, otherwise an array object is returned.\\n516: \\n517:     See Also\\n518:     --------\\n519:     numpy.arccos\\n520: \\n521:     Notes\\n522:     -----\\n523:     For an arccos() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\\n524:     interval ``[-1,1]``, use `numpy.arccos`.\\n525: \\n526:     Examples\\n527:     --------\\n528:     >>> import numpy as np\\n529:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n530: \\n531:     >>> np.emath.arccos(1) # a scalar is returned\\n532:     0.0\\n533: \\n534:     >>> np.emath.arccos([1,2])\\n535:     array([0.-0.j   , 0.-1.317j])\\n536: \\n537:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n538:     x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\\n539:     return nx.arccos(x)\\n540: \\n541: \\n542: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n543: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n544: def arcsin(x):\\n545:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n546:     Compute the inverse sine of x.\\n547: \\n548:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n549:     `numpy.arcsin`) of the inverse sine of `x`. For real `x` such that\\n550:     `abs(x) <= 1`, this is a real number in the closed interval\\n551:     :math:`[-\\\\\\\\pi/2, \\\\\\\\pi/2]`.  Otherwise, the complex principle value is\\n552:     returned.\\n553: \\n554:     Parameters\\n555:     ----------\\n556:     x : array_like or scalar\\n557:        The value(s) whose arcsin is (are) required.\\n558: \\n559:     Returns\\n560:     -------\\n561:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n562:        The inverse sine(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so\\n563:        is `out`, otherwise an array object is returned.\\n564: \\n565:     See Also\\n566:     --------\\n567:     numpy.arcsin\\n568: \\n569:     Notes\\n570:     -----\\n571:     For an arcsin() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\\n572:     interval ``[-1,1]``, use `numpy.arcsin`.\\n573: \\n574:     Examples\\n575:     --------\\n576:     >>> import numpy as np\\n577:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n578: \\n579:     >>> np.emath.arcsin(0)\\n580:     0.0\\n581: \\n582:     >>> np.emath.arcsin([0,1])\\n583:     array([0.    , 1.5708])\\n584: \\n585:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n586:     x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\\n587:     return nx.arcsin(x)\\n588: \\n589: \\n590: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n591: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n592: def arctanh(x):\\n593:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n594:     Compute the inverse hyperbolic tangent of `x`.\\n595: \\n596:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n597:     `numpy.arctanh`) of ``arctanh(x)``. For real `x` such that\\n598:     ``abs(x) < 1``, this is a real number.  If `abs(x) > 1`, or if `x` is\\n599:     complex, the result is complex. Finally, `x = 1` returns``inf`` and\\n600:     ``x=-1`` returns ``-inf``.\\n601: \\n602:     Parameters\\n603:     ----------\\n604:     x : array_like\\n605:        The value(s) whose arctanh is (are) required.\\n606: \\n607:     Returns\\n608:     -------\\n609:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n610:        The inverse hyperbolic tangent(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was\\n611:        a scalar so is `out`, otherwise an array is returned.\\n612: \\n613: \\n614:     See Also\\n615:     --------\\n616:     numpy.arctanh\\n617: \\n618:     Notes\\n619:     -----\\n620:     For an arctanh() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\\n621:     interval ``(-1,1)``, use `numpy.arctanh` (this latter, however, does\\n622:     return +/-inf for ``x = +/-1``).\\n623: \\n624:     Examples\\n625:     --------\\n626:     >>> import numpy as np\\n627:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n628: \\n629:     >>> np.emath.arctanh(0.5)\\n630:     0.5493061443340549\\n631: \\n632:     >>> from numpy.testing import suppress_warnings\\n633:     >>> with suppress_warnings() as sup:\\n634:     ...     sup.filter(RuntimeWarning)\\n635:     ...     np.emath.arctanh(np.eye(2))\\n636:     array([[inf,  0.],\\n637:            [ 0., inf]])\\n638:     >>> np.emath.arctanh([1j])\\n639:     array([0.+0.7854j])\\n640: \\n641:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n642:     x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\\n643:     return nx.arctanh(x)\\n644: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py\"}"
46. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_read_file @ -
Input
{
  "path": "numpy/lib/recfunctions.py",
  "repo": "github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2"
}
Output
"<persisted-output>\nOutput too large (69.1KB). Full output saved to: /logs/agent/sessions/projects/-workspace/e833d333-3081-45c5-9605-5c6c9e917397/tool-results/toolu_01P1Cdwqc7HkMjQ6BBdL4Trq.txt\n\nPreview (first 2KB):\n{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Collection of utilities to manipulate structured arrays.\\n3: \\n4: Most of these functions were initially implemented by John Hunter for\\n5: matplotlib.  They have been rewritten and extended for convenience.\\n6: \\n7: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n8: import itertools\\n9: \\n10: import numpy as np\\n11: import numpy.ma as ma\\n12: import numpy.ma.mrecords as mrec\\n13: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch\\n14: from numpy.lib._iotools import _is_string_like\\n15: \\n16: \\n17: __all__ = [\\n18:     'append_fields', 'apply_along_fields', 'assign_fields_by_name',\\n19:     'drop_fields', 'find_duplicates', 'flatten_descr',\\n20:     'get_fieldstructure', 'get_names', 'get_names_flat',\\n21:     'join_by', 'merge_arrays', 'rec_append_fields',\\n22:     'rec_drop_fields', 'rec_join', 'recursive_fill_fields',\\n23:     'rename_fields', 'repack_fields', 'require_fields',\\n24:     'stack_arrays', 'structured_to_unstructured', 'unstructured_to_structured',\\n25:     ]\\n26: \\n27: \\n28: def _recursive_fill_fields_dispatcher(input, output):\\n29:     return (input, output)\\n30: \\n31: \\n32: @array_function_dispatch(_recursive_fill_fields_dispatcher)\\n33: def recursive_fill_fields(input, output):\\n34:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n35:     Fills fields from output with fields from input,\\n36:     with support for nested structures.\\n37: \\n38:     Parameters\\n39:     ----------\\n40:     input : ndarray\\n41:         Input array.\\n42:     output : ndarray\\n43:         Output array.\\n44: \\n45:     Notes\\n46:     -----\\n47:     * `output` should be at least the same size as `input`\\n48: \\n49:     Examples\\n50:     --------\\n51:     >>> import numpy as np\\n52:     >>> from numpy.lib import recfunctions as rfn\\n53:     >>> a = np.array([(1, 10.), (2, 20.)], dtype=[('A', np.int64), ('B', np.float64)])\\n54:     >>> b = np.zeros((3,), dtype=a.dtype)\\n55:     >>> rfn.recursive_fill_fields(a, b)\\n56:     array([(1, 10.), (2, 20.), (0,  0.)], dtype=[('A', '<i8'), ('B', '<f8')])\\n57: \\n58:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n59\n...\n</persisted-output>"
47. Write @ -
Input
{
  "content": "\"\"\"\nModule of functions that are like ufuncs in acting on arrays and optionally\nstoring results in an output array.\n\n\"\"\"\n__all__ = ['fix', 'isneginf', 'isposinf']\n\nimport numpy._core.numeric as nx\nfrom numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function\n\n\ndef _dispatcher(x, out=None):\n    return (x, out)\n\n\n@dispatch_array_function(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\ndef fix(x, out=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Round to nearest integer towards zero.\n\n    Round an array of floats element-wise to nearest integer towards zero.\n    The rounded values have the same data-type as the input.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n        An array to be rounded\n    out : ndarray, optional\n        A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have\n        a shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\n        freshly-allocated array is returned.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray of floats\n        An array with the same dimensions and data-type as the input.\n        If second argument is not supplied then a new array is returned\n        with the rounded values.\n\n        If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there.\n        The return value ``out`` is then a reference to that array.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    rint, trunc, floor, ceil\n    around : Round to given number of decimals\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.fix(3.14)\n    3.0\n    >>> np.fix(3)\n    3\n    >>> np.fix([2.1, 2.9, -2.1, -2.9])\n    array([ 2.,  2., -2., -2.])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    # promote back to an array if flattened\n    res = nx.asanyarray(nx.ceil(x, out=out))\n    res = nx.floor(x, out=res, where=nx.greater_equal(x, 0))\n\n    # when no out argument is passed and no subclasses are involved, flatten\n    # scalars\n    if out is None and type(res) is nx.ndarray:\n        res = res[()]\n    return res\n\n\n@dispatch_array_function(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\ndef isposinf(x, out=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Test element-wise for positive infinity, return result as bool array.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n        The input array.\n    out : array_like, optional\n        A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have a\n        shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\n        freshly-allocated boolean array is returned.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray\n        A boolean array with the same dimensions as the input.\n        If second argument is not supplied then a boolean array is returned\n        with values True where the corresponding element of the input is\n        positive infinity and values False where the element of the input is\n        not positive infinity.\n\n        If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there. If the\n        type of that array is a numeric type the result is represented as zeros\n        and ones, if the type is boolean then as False and True.\n        The return value `out` is then a reference to that array.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    isinf, isneginf, isfinite, isnan\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic\n    (IEEE 754).\n\n    Errors result if the second argument is also supplied when x is a scalar\n    input, if first and second arguments have different shapes, or if the\n    first argument has complex values\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.isposinf(np.inf)\n    True\n    >>> np.isposinf(-np.inf)\n    False\n    >>> np.isposinf([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\n    array([False, False,  True])\n\n    >>> x = np.array([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\n    >>> y = np.array([2, 2, 2])\n    >>> np.isposinf(x, y)\n    array([0, 0, 1])\n    >>> y\n    array([0, 0, 1])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    is_inf = nx.isinf(x)\n    try:\n        signbit = ~nx.signbit(x)\n    except TypeError as e:\n        dtype = nx.asanyarray(x).dtype\n        raise TypeError(f'This operation is not supported for {dtype} values '\n                        'because it would be ambiguous.') from e\n    else:\n        return nx.logical_and(is_inf, signbit, out)\n\n\n@dispatch_array_function(_dispatcher, verify=False, module='numpy')\ndef isneginf(x, out=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Test element-wise for negative infinity, return result as bool array.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n        The input array.\n    out : array_like, optional\n        A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have a\n        shape that the input broadcasts to. If not provided or None, a\n        freshly-allocated boolean array is returned.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray\n        A boolean array with the same dimensions as the input.\n        If second argument is not supplied then a numpy boolean array is\n        returned with values True where the corresponding element of the\n        input is negative infinity and values False where the element of\n        the input is not negative infinity.\n\n        If a second argument is supplied the result is stored there. If the\n        type of that array is a numeric type the result is represented as\n        zeros and ones, if the type is boolean then as False and True. The\n        return value `out` is then a reference to that array.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    isinf, isposinf, isnan, isfinite\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    NumPy uses the IEEE Standard for Binary Floating-Point for Arithmetic\n    (IEEE 754).\n\n    Errors result if the second argument is also supplied when x is a scalar\n    input, if first and second arguments have different shapes, or if the\n    first argument has complex values.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.isneginf(-np.inf)\n    True\n    >>> np.isneginf(np.inf)\n    False\n    >>> np.isneginf([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\n    array([ True, False, False])\n\n    >>> x = np.array([-np.inf, 0., np.inf])\n    >>> y = np.array([2, 2, 2])\n    >>> np.isneginf(x, y)\n    array([1, 0, 0])\n    >>> y\n    array([1, 0, 0])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    is_inf = nx.isinf(x)\n    try:\n        signbit = nx.signbit(x)\n    except TypeError as e:\n        dtype = nx.asanyarray(x).dtype\n        raise TypeError(f'This operation is not supported for {dtype} values '\n                        'because it would be ambiguous.') from e\n    else:\n        return nx.logical_and(is_inf, signbit, out)\n",
  "file_path": "/workspace/numpy/lib/_ufunclike_impl.py"
}
Output
"File created successfully at: /workspace/numpy/lib/_ufunclike_impl.py"
48. Write @ -
Input
{
  "content": "\"\"\"\nWrapper functions to more user-friendly calling of certain math functions\nwhose output data-type is different than the input data-type in certain\ndomains of the input.\n\nFor example, for functions like `log` with branch cuts, the versions in this\nmodule provide the mathematically valid answers in the complex plane::\n\n  >>> import math\n  >>> np.emath.log(-math.exp(1)) == (1+1j*math.pi)\n  True\n\nSimilarly, `sqrt`, other base logarithms, `power` and trig functions are\ncorrectly handled.  See their respective docstrings for specific examples.\n\n\"\"\"\nimport numpy._core.numeric as nx\nimport numpy._core.numerictypes as nt\nfrom numpy._core.numeric import asarray, any\nfrom numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function, set_module\nfrom numpy.lib._type_check_impl import isreal\n\n\n__all__ = [\n    'sqrt', 'log', 'log2', 'logn', 'log10', 'power', 'arccos', 'arcsin',\n    'arctanh'\n    ]\n\n\n_ln2 = nx.log(2.0)\n\n\ndef _tocomplex(arr):\n    \"\"\"Convert its input `arr` to a complex array.\n\n    The input is returned as a complex array of the smallest type that will fit\n    the original data: types like single, byte, short, etc. become csingle,\n    while others become cdouble.\n\n    A copy of the input is always made.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    arr : array\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    array\n        An array with the same input data as the input but in complex form.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    First, consider an input of type short:\n\n    >>> a = np.array([1,2,3],np.short)\n\n    >>> ac = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(a); ac\n    array([1.+0.j, 2.+0.j, 3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\n\n    >>> ac.dtype\n    dtype('complex64')\n\n    If the input is of type double, the output is correspondingly of the\n    complex double type as well:\n\n    >>> b = np.array([1,2,3],np.double)\n\n    >>> bc = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(b); bc\n    array([1.+0.j, 2.+0.j, 3.+0.j])\n\n    >>> bc.dtype\n    dtype('complex128')\n\n    Note that even if the input was complex to begin with, a copy is still\n    made, since the astype() method always copies:\n\n    >>> c = np.array([1,2,3],np.csingle)\n\n    >>> cc = np.lib.scimath._tocomplex(c); cc\n    array([1.+0.j,  2.+0.j,  3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\n\n    >>> c *= 2; c\n    array([2.+0.j,  4.+0.j,  6.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\n\n    >>> cc\n    array([1.+0.j,  2.+0.j,  3.+0.j], dtype=complex64)\n    \"\"\"\n    if issubclass(arr.dtype.type, (nt.single, nt.byte, nt.short, nt.ubyte,\n                                   nt.ushort, nt.csingle)):\n        return arr.astype(nt.csingle)\n    else:\n        return arr.astype(nt.cdouble)\n\n\ndef _fix_real_lt_zero(x):\n    \"\"\"Convert `x` to complex if it has real, negative components.\n\n    Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    array\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_lt_zero([1,2])\n    array([1, 2])\n\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_lt_zero([-1,2])\n    array([-1.+0.j,  2.+0.j])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = asarray(x)\n    if any(isreal(x) & (x < 0)):\n        x = _tocomplex(x)\n    return x\n\n\ndef _fix_int_lt_zero(x):\n    \"\"\"Convert `x` to double if it has real, negative components.\n\n    Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    array\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_int_lt_zero([1,2])\n    array([1, 2])\n\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_int_lt_zero([-1,2])\n    array([-1.,  2.])\n    \"\"\"\n    x = asarray(x)\n    if any(isreal(x) & (x < 0)):\n        x = x * 1.0\n    return x\n\n\ndef _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x):\n    \"\"\"Convert `x` to complex if it has real components x_i with abs(x_i)>1.\n\n    Otherwise, output is just the array version of the input (via asarray).\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    array\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_abs_gt_1([0,1])\n    array([0, 1])\n\n    >>> np.lib.scimath._fix_real_abs_gt_1([0,2])\n    array([0.+0.j, 2.+0.j])\n    \"\"\"\n    x = asarray(x)\n    if any(isreal(x) & (abs(x) > 1)):\n        x = _tocomplex(x)\n    return x\n\n\ndef _unary_dispatcher(x):\n    return (x,)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef sqrt(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the square root of x.\n\n    For negative input elements, a complex value is returned\n    (unlike `numpy.sqrt` which returns NaN).\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n       The input value(s).\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The square root of `x`. If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\n       otherwise an array is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.sqrt\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    For real, non-negative inputs this works just like `numpy.sqrt`:\n\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt(1)\n    1.0\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt([1, 4])\n    array([1.,  2.])\n\n    But it automatically handles negative inputs:\n\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt(-1)\n    1j\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt([-1,4])\n    array([0.+1.j, 2.+0.j])\n\n    Different results are expected because:\n    floating point 0.0 and -0.0 are distinct.\n\n    For more control, explicitly use complex() as follows:\n\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt(complex(-4.0, 0.0))\n    2j\n    >>> np.emath.sqrt(complex(-4.0, -0.0))\n    -2j\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    return nx.sqrt(x)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef log(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the natural logarithm of `x`.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see `numpy.log`)\n    of :math:`log_e(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this is a real number (``log(0)``\n    returns ``-inf`` and ``log(np.inf)`` returns ``inf``). Otherwise, the\n    complex principle value is returned.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n       The value(s) whose log is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The log of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\n       otherwise an array is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.log\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For a log() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log`\n    (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log` and this `log` are identical,\n    i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`, and,\n    notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.emath.log(np.exp(1))\n    1.0\n\n    Negative arguments are handled \"correctly\" (recall that\n    ``exp(log(x)) == x`` does *not* hold for real ``x < 0``):\n\n    >>> np.emath.log(-np.exp(1)) == (1 + np.pi * 1j)\n    True\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    return nx.log(x)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef log10(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the logarithm base 10 of `x`.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see\n    `numpy.log10`) of :math:`log_{10}(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this\n    is a real number (``log10(0)`` returns ``-inf`` and ``log10(np.inf)``\n    returns ``inf``). Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like or scalar\n       The value(s) whose log base 10 is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The log base 10 of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\n       otherwise an array object is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.log10\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For a log10() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log10`\n    (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log10` and this `log10` are\n    identical, i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`,\n    and, notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n\n    (We set the printing precision so the example can be auto-tested)\n\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.log10(10**1)\n    1.0\n\n    >>> np.emath.log10([-10**1, -10**2, 10**2])\n    array([1.+1.3644j, 2.+1.3644j, 2.+0.j    ])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    return nx.log10(x)\n\n\ndef _logn_dispatcher(n, x):\n    return (n, x,)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_logn_dispatcher)\ndef logn(n, x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Take log base n of x.\n\n    If `x` contains negative inputs, the answer is computed and returned in the\n    complex domain.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    n : array_like\n       The integer base(s) in which the log is taken.\n    x : array_like\n       The value(s) whose log base `n` is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The log base `n` of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is\n       `out`, otherwise an array is returned.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.logn(2, [4, 8])\n    array([2., 3.])\n    >>> np.emath.logn(2, [-4, -8, 8])\n    array([2.+4.5324j, 3.+4.5324j, 3.+0.j    ])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    n = _fix_real_lt_zero(n)\n    return nx.log(x)/nx.log(n)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef log2(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the logarithm base 2 of `x`.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see\n    `numpy.log2`) of :math:`log_2(x)`. For real `x > 0`, this is\n    a real number (``log2(0)`` returns ``-inf`` and ``log2(np.inf)`` returns\n    ``inf``). Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n       The value(s) whose log base 2 is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The log base 2 of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so is `out`,\n       otherwise an array is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.log2\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For a log2() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x < 0`, use `numpy.log2`\n    (note, however, that otherwise `numpy.log2` and this `log2` are\n    identical, i.e., both return ``-inf`` for `x = 0`, ``inf`` for `x = inf`,\n    and, notably, the complex principle value if ``x.imag != 0``).\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n\n    We set the printing precision so the example can be auto-tested:\n\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.log2(8)\n    3.0\n    >>> np.emath.log2([-4, -8, 8])\n    array([2.+4.5324j, 3.+4.5324j, 3.+0.j    ])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    return nx.log2(x)\n\n\ndef _power_dispatcher(x, p):\n    return (x, p)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_power_dispatcher)\ndef power(x, p):\n    \"\"\"\n    Return x to the power p, (x**p).\n\n    If `x` contains negative values, the output is converted to the\n    complex domain.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n        The input value(s).\n    p : array_like of ints\n        The power(s) to which `x` is raised. If `x` contains multiple values,\n        `p` has to either be a scalar, or contain the same number of values\n        as `x`. In the latter case, the result is\n        ``x[0]**p[0], x[1]**p[1], ...``.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n        The result of ``x**p``. If `x` and `p` are scalars, so is `out`,\n        otherwise an array is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.power\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.power(2, 2)\n    4\n\n    >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], 2)\n    array([ 4, 16])\n\n    >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], -2)\n    array([0.25  ,  0.0625])\n\n    >>> np.emath.power([-2, 4], 2)\n    array([ 4.-0.j, 16.+0.j])\n\n    >>> np.emath.power([2, 4], [2, 4])\n    array([ 4, 256])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_lt_zero(x)\n    p = _fix_int_lt_zero(p)\n    return nx.power(x, p)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef arccos(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the inverse cosine of x.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see\n    `numpy.arccos`) of the inverse cosine of `x`. For real `x` such that\n    `abs(x) <= 1`, this is a real number in the closed interval\n    :math:`[0, \\\\pi]`.  Otherwise, the complex principle value is returned.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like or scalar\n       The value(s) whose arccos is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The inverse cosine(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so\n       is `out`, otherwise an array object is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.arccos\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For an arccos() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\n    interval ``[-1,1]``, use `numpy.arccos`.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.arccos(1) # a scalar is returned\n    0.0\n\n    >>> np.emath.arccos([1,2])\n    array([0.-0.j   , 0.-1.317j])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\n    return nx.arccos(x)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef arcsin(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the inverse sine of x.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see\n    `numpy.arcsin`) of the inverse sine of `x`. For real `x` such that\n    `abs(x) <= 1`, this is a real number in the closed interval\n    :math:`[-\\\\pi/2, \\\\pi/2]`.  Otherwise, the complex principle value is\n    returned.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like or scalar\n       The value(s) whose arcsin is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The inverse sine(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was a scalar, so\n       is `out`, otherwise an array object is returned.\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.arcsin\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For an arcsin() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\n    interval ``[-1,1]``, use `numpy.arcsin`.\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.arcsin(0)\n    0.0\n\n    >>> np.emath.arcsin([0,1])\n    array([0.    , 1.5708])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\n    return nx.arcsin(x)\n\n\n@set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\n@dispatch_array_function(_unary_dispatcher)\ndef arctanh(x):\n    \"\"\"\n    Compute the inverse hyperbolic tangent of `x`.\n\n    Return the \"principal value\" (for a description of this, see\n    `numpy.arctanh`) of ``arctanh(x)``. For real `x` such that\n    ``abs(x) < 1``, this is a real number.  If `abs(x) > 1`, or if `x` is\n    complex, the result is complex. Finally, `x = 1` returns``inf`` and\n    ``x=-1`` returns ``-inf``.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    x : array_like\n       The value(s) whose arctanh is (are) required.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    out : ndarray or scalar\n       The inverse hyperbolic tangent(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was\n       a scalar so is `out`, otherwise an array is returned.\n\n\n    See Also\n    --------\n    numpy.arctanh\n\n    Notes\n    -----\n    For an arctanh() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\n    interval ``(-1,1)``, use `numpy.arctanh` (this latter, however, does\n    return +/-inf for ``x = +/-1``).\n\n    Examples\n    --------\n    >>> import numpy as np\n    >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\n\n    >>> np.emath.arctanh(0.5)\n    0.5493061443340549\n\n    >>> from numpy.testing import suppress_warnings\n    >>> with suppress_warnings() as sup:\n    ...     sup.filter(RuntimeWarning)\n    ...     np.emath.arctanh(np.eye(2))\n    array([[inf,  0.],\n           [ 0., inf]])\n    >>> np.emath.arctanh([1j])\n    array([0.+0.7854j])\n\n    \"\"\"\n    x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\n    return nx.arctanh(x)\n",
  "file_path": "/workspace/numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py"
}
Output
"File created successfully at: /workspace/numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py"

Full output saved to: /logs/agent/sessions/projects/-workspace/e833d333-3081-45c5-9605-5c6c9e917397/tool-results/toolu_01DLPobQzkio6WAV3heUadfJ.txt\n\nPreview (first 2KB):\n     1\u2192{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Collection of utilities to manipulate structured arrays.\\n3: \\n4: Most of these functions were initially implemented by John Hunter for\\n5: matplotlib.  They have been rewritten and extended for convenience.\\n6: \\n7: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n8: import itertools\\n9: \\n10: import numpy as np\\n11: import numpy.ma as ma\\n12: import numpy.ma.mrecords as mrec\\n13: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch\\n14: from numpy.lib._iotools import _is_string_like\\n15: \\n16: \\n17: __all__ = [\\n18:     'append_fields', 'apply_along_fields', 'assign_fields_by_name',\\n19:     'drop_fields', 'find_duplicates', 'flatten_descr',\\n20:     'get_fieldstructure', 'get_names', 'get_names_flat',\\n21:     'join_by', 'merge_arrays', 'rec_append_fields',\\n22:     'rec_drop_fields', 'rec_join', 'recursive_fill_fields',\\n23:     'rename_fields', 'repack_fields', 'require_fields',\\n24:     'stack_arrays', 'structured_to_unstructured', 'unstructured_to_structured',\\n25:     ]\\n26: \\n27: \\n28: def _recursive_fill_fields_dispatcher(input, output):\\n29:     return (input, output)\\n30: \\n31: \\n32: @array_function_dispatch(_recursive_fill_fields_dispatcher)\\n33: def recursive_fill_fields(input, output):\\n34:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n35:     Fills fields from output with fields from input,\\n36:     with support for nested structures.\\n37: \\n38:     Parameters\\n39:     ----------\\n40:     input : ndarray\\n41:         Input array.\\n42:     output : ndarray\\n43:         Output array.\\n44: \\n45:     Notes\\n46:     -----\\n47:     * `output` should be at least the same size as `input`\\n48: \\n49:     Examples\\n50:     --------\\n51:     >>> import numpy as np\\n52:     >>> from numpy.lib import recfunctions as rfn\\n53:     >>> a = np.array([(1, 10.), (2, 20.)], dtype=[('A', np.int64), ('B', np.float64)])\\n54:     >>> b = np.zeros((3,), dtype=a.dtype)\\n55:     >>> rfn.recursive_fill_fields(a, b)\\n56:     array([(1, 10.), (2, 20.), (0,  0.)], dtype=[('A', '<i8'), ('B', '<f8')])\\n57: \\n58:     \\\"\\\n...\n</persisted-output>"

get_overridable_numpy_array_functions\\n15: from numpy._core.overrides import (\\n16:     _get_implementing_args, array_function_dispatch,\\n17:     verify_matching_signatures)\\n18: \\n19: def test_ndarray(self):\\n39:         array = np.array(1)\\n40: \\n41:         args = _get_implementing_args([array])\\n42:         assert_equal(list(args), [array])\\n43: \\n44:         args = [override_sub, no_override_sub])\\n74: \\n75:     def test_ndarray_and_duck_array(self):\\n76: \\n77:         class Other:\\n78:             __array_function__ = _return_not_implemented\\n79: \\n80: 53. Write @ -
Input
{
  "content": "from .common import Benchmark\n\ntry:\n    from numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function\nexcept ImportError:\n    # Don't fail at import time with old Numpy versions\n    def dispatch_array_function(*args, **kwargs):\n        def wrap(*args, **kwargs):\n            return None\n        return wrap\n\nimport numpy as np\n\n\ndef _broadcast_to_dispatcher(array, shape, subok=None):\n    return (array,)\n\n\n@dispatch_array_function(_broadcast_to_dispatcher)\ndef mock_broadcast_to(array, shape, subok=False):\n    pass\n\n\ndef _concatenate_dispatcher(arrays, axis=None, out=None):\n    if out is not None:\n        arrays = list(arrays)\n        arrays.append(out)\n    return arrays\n\n\n@dispatch_array_function(_concatenate_dispatcher)\ndef mock_concatenate(arrays, axis=0, out=None):\n    pass\n\n\nclass DuckArray:\n    def __array_function__(self, func, types, args, kwargs):\n        pass\n\n\nclass ArrayFunction(Benchmark):\n\n    def setup(self):\n        self.numpy_array = np.array(1)\n        self.numpy_arrays = [np.array(1), np.array(2)]\n        self.many_arrays = 500 * self.numpy_arrays\n        self.duck_array = DuckArray()\n        self.duck_arrays = [DuckArray(), DuckArray()]\n        self.mixed_arrays = [np.array(1), DuckArray()]\n\n    def time_mock_broadcast_to_numpy(self):\n        mock_broadcast_to(self.numpy_array, ())\n\n    def time_mock_broadcast_to_duck(self):\n        mock_broadcast_to(self.duck_array, ())\n\n    def time_mock_concatenate_numpy(self):\n        mock_concatenate(self.numpy_arrays, axis=0)\n\n    def time_mock_concatenate_many(self):\n        mock_concatenate(self.many_arrays, axis=0)\n\n    def time_mock_concatenate_duck(self):\n        mock_concatenate(self.duck_arrays, axis=0)\n\n    def time_mock_concatenate_mixed(self):\n        mock_concatenate(self.mixed_arrays, axis=0)\n",
  "file_path": "/workspace/benchmarks/benchmarks/bench_overrides.py"
}
Output
"File created successfully at: /workspace/benchmarks/benchmarks/bench_overrides.py"

types'):\\n144:             _get_implementing_args(relevant_args)\\n145: \\n146: \\n147: class TestNDArrayArrayFunction:\\n148: \\n149:     def test_method(self):\\n150: \\n151:         class Other:\\n152:             __array_function__ = _return_not_implemented\\n153: \\n154:         class NoOverrideSub(np.ndarray):\\n155:             pass\\n156: \\n157:         class OverrideSub(np.ndarray):\\n158:             __array_function__ = _return_not_implemented\\n159: \\n160:         array = np.array([1])\\n161:         other = Other()\\n162:         no_override_sub = array.view(NoOverrideSub)\\n163:         override_sub = array.view(OverrideSub)\\n164: \\n165:         result = array.__array_function__(func=dispatched_two_arg,\\n166: array.__array_function__(func=dispatched_two_arg,\\n171:                                           types=(np.ndarray, Other),\\n172:                                           args=(array, other), kwargs={})\\n173:         assert_(result is NotImplemented)\\n174: \\n175:         result = array.__array_function__(func=dispatched_two_arg,\\n176:                                           types=(np.ndarray, NoOverrideSub),\\n177:                                           args=(array, no_override_sub),\\n178:                                           kwargs={})\\n179:         assert_equal(result, 'original')\\n180: \\n181:         result = array.__array_function__(func=dispatched_two_arg,\\n182:                                           types=(np.ndarray, OverrideSub),\\n183:                                           args=(array, override_sub),\\n184:                                           kwargs={})\\n185:         assert_equal(result, 'original')\\n186: \\n187:         with assert_raises_regex(TypeError, 'no implementation found'):\\n188:             np.concatenate((array, other))\\n189: \\n190:         expected = np.concatenate((array, array))\\n191:         result = np.concatenate((array, no_override_sub))\\n192:         assert_equal(result, expected.view(NoOverrideSub))\\n193:         result = np.concatenate((array, override_sub))\\n194:         assert_equal(result, expected.view(OverrideSub))\\n195: \\n196:     def test_no_wrapper(self):\\n197:         # Regular numpy functions have wrappers, but do not presume\\n198:         # all functions do (array creation ones do not): check that\\n199:         # we just call the function in that case.\\n200:         array = np.array(1)\\n201:         func = lambda x: x * 2\\n202:         result = array.__array_function__(func=func, types=(np.ndarray,),\\n203:                                           args=(array,), kwargs={})\\n204:         assert_equal(result, array * 2)\\n205: \\n206:     def test_wrong_arguments(self):\\n207:         # Check our implementation guards against wrong arguments.\\n208:         a = np.array([1, 2])\\n209:         with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=\\\"args must be a tuple\\\"):\\n210:             a.__array_function__(np.reshape, (np.ndarray,), a, (2, 1))\\n211:         with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=\\\"kwargs must be a dict\\\"):\\n212:             a.__array_function__(np.reshape, (np.ndarray,), (a,), (2, 1))\\n213: \\n214:     def test_wrong_arguments(self):\\n215:         # Check our implementation guards against wrong arguments.\\n216:         a = np.array([1, 2])\\n217:         with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=\\\"args must be a tuple\\\"):\\n218:             a.__array_function__(np.reshape, (np.ndarray,), a, (2, 1))\\n219:         with pytest.raises(TypeError, match=\\\"kwargs must be a dict\\\"):\\n220:             a.__array_function__(np.reshape, (np.ndarray,), (a,), (2, 1))\\n221: \\n222: \\n223: class TestArrayFunctionDispatch:\\n224: \\n225:     def test_pickle(self):\\n226:         for proto in range(2, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL + 1):\\n227:             roundtripped = pickle.loads(\\n228:                     pickle.dumps(dispatched_one_arg, protocol=proto))\\n229:             assert_(roundtripped is dispatched_one_arg)\\n230: \\n231:     def test_name_and_docstring(self):\\n232:         assert_equal(dispatched_one_arg.__name__, 'dispatched_one_arg')\\n233:         if sys.flags.optimize < 2:\\n234:             assert_equal(dispatched_one_arg.__doc__, 'Docstring.')\\n235: \\n236:     def test_interface(self):\\n237: \\n238:         class MyArray:\\n239:             def __array_function__(self, func, types, args, kwargs):\\n240:                 return (self, func, types, args, kwargs)\\n241: \\n242:         original = MyArray()\\n243:         (obj, func, types, args, kwargs) = dispatched_one_arg(original)\\n244:         assert_(obj is original)\\n245:         assert_(func is dispatched_one_arg)\\n246:         assert_equal(set(types), {MyArray})\\n247:         # assert_equal uses the overloaded np.iscomplexobj() internally\\n248:         assert_(args == (original,))\\n249:         assert_equal(kwargs, {})\\n250: \\n251:     def test_not_implemented(self):\\n252: \\n253:         class MyArray:\\n254:             def __array_function__(self, func, types, args, kwargs):\\n255:                 return NotImplemented\\n256: \\n257:         array = MyArray()\\n258:         with assert_raises_regex(TypeError, 'no implementation found'):\\n259:             dispatched_one_arg(array)\\n260: \\n261:     def test_where_dispatch(self):\\n262: \\n263:         class DuckArray:\\n264:             def __array_function__(self, ufunc, method, *inputs, **kwargs):\\n265:                 return \\\"overridden\\\"\\n266: \\n267:         array = np.array(1)\\n268:         duck_array = DuckArray()\\n269: \\n270:         result = np.std(array, where=duck_array)\\n271: \\n272:         assert_equal(result, \\\"overridden\\\")\\n273: \\n274: \\n275: class TestVerifyMatchingSignatures:\\n276: \\n277:     def test_verify_matching_signatures(self):\\n278: \\n279:         verify_matching_signatures(lambda x: 0, lambda x: 0)\\n280:         verify_matching_signatures(lambda x=None: 0, lambda x=None: 0)\\n281:         verify_matching_signatures(lambda x=1: 0, lambda x=None: 0)\\n282: \\n283:         with assert_raises(RuntimeError):\\n284:             verify_matching_signatures(lambda a: 0, lambda b: 0)\\n285:         with assert_raises(RuntimeError):\\n286:             verify_matching_signatures(lambda x: 0, lambda x=None: 0)\\n287:         with assert_raises(RuntimeError):\\n288:             verify_matching_signatures(lambda x=None: 0, lambda y=None: 0)\\n289:         with assert_raises(RuntimeError):\\n290:             verify_matching_signatures(lambda x=1: 0, lambda y=1: 0)\\n291: \\n292:     def test_array_function_dispatch(self):\\n293: \\n294:         with assert_raises(RuntimeError):\\n295:             @array_function_dispatch(lambda x: (x,))\\n296:             def f(y):\\n297:                 pass\\n298: \\n299:         # should not raise\\n300:         @array_function_dispatch(lambda x: (x,), verify=False)\\n301:
